MINI MINUTES FOR JUL-15-2016 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT NATIONAL RAILROAD PASSENGER CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF KERRY PEREZ'S FOURTH AMENDED COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED. PLAINTIFF HAS ADEQUATELY ALLEGED WHY HE BELIEVES HE WAS IN FEAR OF BODILY HARM, WHICH WAS THE BASIS FOR GRANTING HIM LEAVE TO AMEND. PLAINTIFF STATES A COGNIZABLE CLAIM FOR REASONABLE FEAR OF IMMINENT BODILY HARM BY ALLEGING THAT HE WAS AFRAID OF BEING STRUCK BY PARTS OF THE CROSSING GATE, DECEDENT'S BICYCLE AND DECEDENT HIMSELF. A PERSON WHO INVESTIGATES A RECENT TRAIN CRASH MAY BE IN THE ZONE OF DANGER. (LEE V. NATIONAL RAILROAD PASSENGER CORP. (S.D. MISS. 2011) 791 F.SUPP.2D 550, 552.) BLOOM V. CONSOL. RAIL CORP. (1994) 41 F.3D 911 (COLLISION WITH PERSON) AND LUKOWSKI V. CSX TRANSP., INC. (6TH CIR. 2005) 416 F.3D 478, 480 (COLLISION WITH TRUCK), RELIED ON BY DEFENDANT ARE DISTINGUISHABLE ON THEIR FACTS. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: JOSE RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: REYES F. HUNTER, CSR #6576, BUS. TEL: 530-339-5023. (D302)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant National Railroad Passenger Corporation Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar May-24-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Vincent Castillo, of Lombardi, Loper & Conant, LLP, Tel. 510.433.2600, for defendants Amtrak;
Brad M. Weintraub, of Gordon & Rees LLP, Tel. 415.986.5900, for defendants Transitamerica Services, Inc.

Ruling: The matter was not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT NATIONAL RAILROAD PASSENGER CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT held. Under the FELA, a plaintiff may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress only if the plaintiff sustained a physical injury as a result of defendant's negligence or was placed in immediate risk of physical impact or harm by that conduct. (Consolidated Rail Corp. v. Gottshall (1994) 512 U.S. 532, 547-548, 556.) Under the zone of danger test, "a worker within the zone of danger of physical impact will be able to recover for emotional injury caused by fear of physical injury to himself, whereas a worker outside the zone will not." (Id. at p. 556.) Here, the undisputed evidence is that plaintiff did not sustain any physical impact or injury. (UMF 21). The question therefore is whether he was in the zone of danger. Defendant has produced evidence of undisputed facts that defendant was not negligent and plaintiff was not in the zone of danger. (UMF 16, 17, 19.) The undisputed evidence is that decedent ducked under a completely descended crossing arm and ran between cars of the moving train. (UMF 8, 9). The sole cause of this incident therefore was decedent's conduct, not any negligence on defendant's part. Plaintiff testified that as soon as he realized the train was traveling northbound, he "ran and got out of there." (UMF 19). Although plaintiff also declared that he experienced imminent fear of being struck by a portion of the crossing gate, a part of the decedent's body, or decedent's bicycle, he was not in the zone of danger. (UMF 20, 21, 23, 24). The plaintiff does not dispute that decedent was north of plaintiff at the time of impact, about fifty feet away from plaintiff. (UMF 20, 23). Plaintiff's alleged dispute of material facts 23 and 24 are not genuine disputes. All items of evidence, including decedent's body, blood and arm, were found north of the point of impact except for decedent's backpack which was found at the side of the area of impact. Since plaintiff was south of the area of impact, he was not in a zone of danger. (UMF 24). See Bloom v. Consolidated Rail Corp. (3d Cir. 1994) 41 F.3d 911, 917 (a train conductor who is inside a train when it hits a person is not in the zone of danger); Lukowski v. CSX Transp., Inc. (6th Cir. 2005) 416 F.3d 478, 480 (a train conductor who is inside a train when it hits a truck is not in the zone of danger). Defendant National Railroad Passenger Corporation's (Amtrak) motion for summary judgment on the sole cause of action alleged against it for violation of the Federal Employer's Liability Act is therefore GRANTED. The Court overrules defendant's objections to plaintiff's evidence numbers 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8. The Court sustains defendant's objection to plaintiff's evidence numbers 1, 2 (with regard to statement "Per the attached page from the Sheriff's report") and 9. Order signed. Judge: Stephen Murphy; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189; Dept. 302.

Off Calendar Jul-15-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Attorney, George S. Duesdieker, Esq. (650-566-9529) appears for plaintiff/opposing party Kerry Perez

Attorney, Vincent Castillo of Lombardi Loper & Conant, LLP (510-433-2600) appears for defendant/demurring party, National Railroad Passenger Corporation (Amtrak)

The cause is argued by both sides. The Court confirms the tentative ruling. DEFENDANT NATIONAL RAILROAD PASSENGER CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF KERRY PEREZ'S FOURTH AMENDED COMPLAINT is OVERRULED. Plaintiff has adequately alleged why he believes he was in fear of bodily harm, which was the basis for granting him leave to amend. Plaintiff states a cognizable claim for reasonable fear of imminent bodily harm by alleging that he was afraid of being struck by parts of the crossing gate, decedent's bicycle and decedent himself. A person who investigates a recent train crash may be in the zone of danger. (Lee v. National Railroad Passenger Corp. (S.D. Miss. 2011) 791 F.Supp.2d 550, 552.) Bloom v. Consol. Rail Corp. (1994) 41 F.3d 911 (collision with person) and Lukowski v. CSX Transp., Inc. (6th Cir. 2005) 416 F.3d 478, 480 (collision with truck), relied on by defendant are distinguishable on their facts. Written order is signed and filed in open court.
